companyCaseStudy.title: How Zoom Scaled Real‑Time Video from ~10M to 200M Daily Participants — Keeping Latency Low While Traffic Exploded
companyCaseStudy.summary: In under four months in early 2020 Zoom grew from roughly 10 million daily meeting participants to about 200 million — a ~20x surge — and met it by redesigning the media plane, automating relay deployment, and prioritizing low‑latency routing. This case study walks through the problem, key architectural choices (SFU/TURN/P2P), and concrete results.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook

In December 2019 Zoom had ~10 million daily meeting participants. By March 2020 that number ballooned to roughly 200 million daily participants — and meeting minutes surged into the billions per day. Suddenly every part of the system that touches media had to scale 10–20x without turning every meeting into a laggy call.

## Problem

- Explosive growth: ~20x increase in daily participants in months.  
- Real‑time constraints: sub‑200ms audio round‑trip ideal; video resolution and frame rate sensitive to packet loss.  
- NATs, firewalls, and mobile networks: required TURN relays and efficient NAT traversal.  
- Cost & capacity: media servers are CPU and bandwidth intensive; naive scaling would be prohibitively expensive.

## Challenges

- Multiparty video naively requires O(n^2) bandwidth if every client sends to every client.  
- Deploying media servers (bridges/relays) globally and routing participants to the best one under variable network conditions.  
- Keeping signaling stateless enough to autoscale, while maintaining session consistency for meetings.  
- Handling extreme bursts (daytime in one region) without global overload or catastrophic failures.

## Solution (what Zoom actually did)

Zoom focused on separating concerns and optimizing the media plane:

1. Media plane model: P2P for 1:1 calls, SFU (selective forwarding unit / media relay) for multi‑party. SFU means each client uploads only one stream to the relay and downloads mixed/selectively forwarded streams — drastically lowering upload counts and client CPU.

2. Global edge POPs + Media Relays: Deploy hundreds/thousands of media relay instances across many regions (edge POPs). Each meeting is anchored to a regional media relay chosen by a relay‑selection service that considers RTT, packet loss, and load.

3. NAT traversal & fallbacks: Use STUN for direct connectivity, TURN relays when P2P fails, and TURN over UDP/TCP as fallback. TURN capacity was rapidly expanded and automated.

4. Adaptive media: Prioritize audio over video, use adaptive bitrate and frame‑rate switching, use Forward Error Correction (FEC) and NACKs to reduce perceived packet loss, and employ codec tuning for low CPU and good quality at varying bitrates.

5. Stateless signaling + session manager: Signaling frontends were kept mostly stateless (scale horizontally behind load balancers). Session state (which media relay a meeting uses) is stored in a small session service so frontends can scale and fail without losing meetings.

6. Autoscaling & fast provisioning: Automate provisioning of media relays and TURN capacity (cloud + co‑located servers). Use metrics (CPU, network, packet loss) to trigger scale‑out and scale‑in.

7. Prioritized traffic & graceful degradation: Audio gets highest priority (to avoid participants being cut off). Video quality is reduced first when congestion is detected.

### Architecture (simplified)

```mermaid
graph LR
  ClientA[Client A (Web/Mobile)] -->|Signaling (WS/HTTPS)| LB[Load Balancer]
  LB --> Signaling[Signaling Frontend]
  Signaling --> SessionSvc[Session Service]
  SessionSvc --> RelaySelector[Relay Selection]
  RelaySelector --> MediaRelay1[Media Relay (SFU) - Region 1]
  RelaySelector --> MediaRelay2[Media Relay (SFU) - Region 2]
  ClientA -->|Media (UDP/TURN)| MediaRelay1
  ClientB -->|Media (UDP/TURN)| MediaRelay1
  ClientC -->|Media (UDP/TURN)| MediaRelay2
  MediaRelay1 -->|Forward streams| ClientB
  MediaRelay1 -->|Forward streams| ClientA
```

## Before vs After (high level)

| Metric | Before (Dec 2019) | Peak (Mar 2020) |
|--------|-------------------:|---------------:|
| Daily meeting participants | ~10,000,000 | ~200,000,000 |
| Meeting minutes / day | hundreds of millions | billions+ |
| Media relay fleet size | hundreds (estimate) | thousands / tens of thousands (autoscaled) |
| Typical per‑meeting outbound streams | O(n) (client) | 1 upload → SFU forwards selected streams |

> Note: Zoom published participant numbers publicly (the jump from ~10M to ~200M daily participants in early 2020 was widely reported); infrastructure counts are from engineering statements and public interviews describing massive, automated scale‑up of media relays and TURN capacity.

## Results — what the architecture achieved

- Operable at 10–20x growth without massive service interruptions.  
- Kept audio as first‑class traffic and implemented graceful degradation so meetings were usable even on poor networks.  
- Reduced client upload complexity: clients send a single stream to a regionally‑selected relay (rather than many uploads), lowering client CPU/bandwidth usage and improving battery life for mobile.

## What engineering tradeoffs were made

- SFU vs MCU: SFU keeps CPU per meeting lower on server side than a full decode‑mix‑encode MCU, but requires clients to do more decoding (they may receive multiple streams). Netflix‑style full transcoding (MCU) would have higher server cost.
- Global POPs cost more but reduced cross‑region hops and improved latency.  
- TURN capacity is expensive (bandwidth + egress), but unavoidable to traverse restrictive NATs.

## Practical patterns you can reuse

- Separate signaling from media. Keep signaling stateless so it can autoscale quickly.  
- Use SFU for multi‑party to turn O(n^2) bandwidth into O(n) uploads + efficient relay forwarding.  
- Automate TURN and media relay provisioning; treat them as elastic infrastructure.  
- Prioritize audio and add graceful degradation paths for video quality.

## Comparison: Options at a glance

| Option | Pros | Cons |
|--------|------|------|
| P2P (1:1) | Lowest server bandwidth, minimal latency | Fails with restrictive NATs, not suitable for large groups |
| SFU (chosen) | Scales well for groups, lower server CPU than MCU, simpler to autoscale | Clients may receive many streams (decode cost), complexity in relay selection |
| MCU | Clients receive a single mixed stream, easier bitrate control | Very high server CPU / cost, not as elastic for large spikes |

## Example: Relay selection pseudocode

```go
// Simplified relay selection logic (pseudocode)
func selectRelay(clientMetrics, candidateRelays) Relay {
  // score combines RTT, packetLoss, and relayLoad
  best := candidateRelays[0]
  bestScore := scoreRelay(best, clientMetrics)
  for _, r := range candidateRelays[1:] {
    s := scoreRelay(r, clientMetrics)
    if s < bestScore { best, bestScore = r, s }
  }
  return best
}

func scoreRelay(r Relay, m ClientMetrics) float64 {
  // weights are tuned: latency most important, then loss, then load
  return 0.6*normalize(r.rtt, m.rtt) + 0.3*r.packetLoss + 0.1*r.cpuLoad
}
```

## Final notes

Zoom's rapid scaling in early 2020 is a practical example of focusing on the media plane: choose the right forwarding architecture (SFU where appropriate), invest in global relays and NAT traversal, automate capacity, and always prioritize the user experience (audio first). These principles apply whether you're building a small conferencing feature or a platform serving hundreds of millions of daily participants.
keyTakeaways: Separate signaling from the media plane: makes autoscaling and failover much easier.
Use SFU for multi‑party calls to avoid O(n^2) bandwidth/cpu on clients and limit server encode costs.
Automate and elasticize TURN/media relay capacity — real‑time systems must be able to scale globally under sudden ~10–20x growth.
companyCaseStudy.codeExample: func selectRelay(clientMetrics, candidateRelays) Relay { /* choose relay by RTT, loss, load using weighted scoring (pseudocode shown in explanation) */ }

concept.title: Write Time Travel: Event Sourcing + CQRS for Auditability, Scale, and Faster Reads
concept.summary: Event Sourcing turns every state change into an immutable event (a ledger) so you can rebuild state, audit, and ask temporal questions. CQRS splits writes (commands) from reads (queries) so you can scale and optimize each side independently — together they give traceability and read-performance, at the cost of added complexity.
concept.explanation:
## Why this matters

Imagine trying to answer: "What exactly happened to order #123?" or "What was the customer balance on 2024-01-01?" With traditional CRUD, that can be hard unless you designed for it up front. **Event Sourcing** solves this by treating every change as an immutable event — a ledger you can replay. **CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)** complements it by separating the *intent to change* (commands/writes) from *fast queries* (reads), letting you scale and shape read models for UX without polluting your transactional logic.

**Analogy:** Event Sourcing is a bank ledger — every transaction is recorded. CQRS is like having a teller system (writes) and a customer-facing balance board (read model) optimized for speed and display.

---

## Quick visual: how they fit together

Mermaid flowchart (high level):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Client-->API[API/Command Handler]
  API-->Validator{Validate}
  Validator-->|OK| EventStore[Append Event to Event Store]
  EventStore-->Projector[Projectors/Processors]
  Projector-->ReadDB[Read Model (optimized DB)]
  Client-->QueryAPI[Query API]
  QueryAPI-->ReadDB
```

Mermaid sequence diagram (timeline of a command + read):

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  participant C as Client
  participant API as Command API
  participant ES as Event Store
  participant P as Projector
  participant R as Read Model
  C->>API: CreateOrder(command)
  API->>ES: Append(OrderCreated)
  ES-->>API: Ack
  ES->>P: Stream new events
  P->>R: Update projections
  C->>QueryAPI: GetOrderSummary(query)
  QueryAPI->>R: Read
  R-->>QueryAPI: Fast response
  QueryAPI-->>C: Return
```

---

## Before / After (crud vs event-sourcing + cqrs)

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| CRUD (single DB) | Simple, familiar, strong consistency | Hard to audit, difficult to answer temporal queries, read scale tied to write DB | Small apps, simple domains, small teams |
| Event Sourcing + CQRS | Full audit trail, rebuild past states, optimized read models, easier to scale reads independently | More moving parts, eventual consistency, schema evolution of events | Complex domains, audit/financial domains, high read/write disagreement |

---

## How it works (practical): events, aggregates, projections

- Write path:
  1. Client sends a *Command* (intention), e.g., CreateOrder.
  2. Command handler loads an *Aggregate* (reconstructed by replaying events) and asks it to handle the command.
  3. The aggregate emits one or more *Events* (OrderCreated, ItemAdded).
  4. Events are **appended** to the event store (immutable log).
  5. The system acknowledges the command once events are durably stored.

- Read path:
  1. Projectors / subscribers consume events from the event store and update one or more **read models** (SQL, NoSQL, search index).
  2. Clients query these read models — optimized for specific queries and latency.

Important: reads are often *eventually consistent* with writes because projection is asynchronous.

---

## Minimal example (Node.js / JavaScript) — in-memory event store and replay

```js
// Very small illustrative example
class EventStore {
  constructor() { this.events = []; }
  append(event) { this.events.push({ id: this.events.length, ...event }); }
  getStream(aggregateId) { return this.events.filter(e => e.aggregateId === aggregateId); }
}

// Aggregate reconstructs state by replaying events
function reconstructOrder(events) {
  return events.reduce((state, e) => {
    switch(e.type) {
      case 'OrderCreated': return { id: e.aggregateId, items: [], status: 'open' };
      case 'ItemAdded': return { ...state, items: state.items.concat(e.payload) };
      case 'OrderPaid': return { ...state, status: 'paid' };
      default: return state;
    }
  }, {});
}

// Usage
const store = new EventStore();
store.append({ aggregateId: 'order-1', type: 'OrderCreated', payload: {} });
store.append({ aggregateId: 'order-1', type: 'ItemAdded', payload: { sku: 'S1', qty: 2 } });
const events = store.getStream('order-1');
console.log(reconstructOrder(events));
```

Add snapshotting in production when replaying long streams.

---

## When to use this combo (practical guidance)

Use Event Sourcing + CQRS when:
- You need a complete audit trail or legal/financial record.
- You must answer temporal queries: "what did the system look like at time T?"
- You have many different read shapes or need specialized read stores (e.g., SQL for dashboards, search index for lookup).
- You need to scale read workloads independently of write throughput.

Avoid when:
- Your domain is simple CRUD with little business logic.
- Team unfamiliarity would make maintenance risky.
- Strong immediate consistency across many read models is a hard requirement (possible but costly).

---

## Common pitfalls and how to mitigate them

1. Event schema evolution (changing event shape):
   - Use versioned events, upcasters, or compatibility layers.
   - Keep events as small, explicit facts (avoid embedding large object graphs).

2. Debugging & Tooling: replaying events can be opaque.
   - Build good tooling: event inspectors, replays, and correlation IDs.

3. Event store performance & storage growth:
   - Use snapshotting for aggregates with long event streams.
   - Archive or compress old events where business rules allow.

4. Eventual consistency surprises:
   - Surface "staleness" in UI or provide read-after-write paths for critical flows (e.g., synchronous projection or a short-term cache).

5. Overuse of events for trivial apps:
   - Don’t design with EventSourcing because it’s trendy — measure the benefits against added operational cost.

---

## Real examples and patterns

- Many payment and trading systems use Event Sourcing for auditability.
- Complex domains (order processing, warehouse management) use CQRS to have one write model and many read models (inventory view, customer view, reporting).

---

## Quick checklist to get started

- Identify aggregates that own invariants (e.g., Order, Account).
- Define domain events (past-tense facts): OrderCreated, ItemAdded, PaymentReceived.
- Build an append-only event store (durable, ordered). Start simple (DB table) then evolve.
- Add projectors to populate read models tailored to queries.
- Add monitoring for projection lag and tooling for event inspection and replay.

---

## TL;DR

Event Sourcing gives you a full, replayable history. CQRS lets you shape read models for fast queries. Together they're powerful for auditability and scale, but they add complexity: plan for schema evolution, tooling, and eventual consistency.
keyTakeaways: Event store is the immutable source of truth — you can rebuild any state by replaying events.
CQRS separates write and read concerns so you can optimize and scale them independently, but expect eventual consistency.
Plan for event schema evolution, projection lag, snapshotting, and developer tooling before adopting in production.
